question: What does phobethete ton theon ('fear God') mean as the first imperative of the everlasting gospel? How does the OT yare/phobos tradition inform this command — is it servile terror or reverent awe? How does Deuteronomy 6:13 and 10:12-13 pair fear and love? Does 'fear God' conflict with 'perfect love casts out fear' (1 John 4:18)? Parse the 1 John 4:18 text carefully — what does phobos meta kolaseos ('fear with punishment') mean, and is that the same kind of fear as Rev 14:7? How does fearing God function as the foundation for refusing to fear the beast and his threats (Rev 13:15, Matt 10:28)? How does Ecclesiastes 12:13 ('Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man') provide the OT template for the first angel's message?

topics.name: FEAR
topics.entry_text: FEAR — CONSPICUOUS INSTANCES OF THOSE WHO FEARED: Noah (HEB 11:7), Abraham tested in offering Isaac (GEN 22:12), Jacob vision (GEN 28:16-17; 42:18), Midwives of Egypt (EXO 1:17,21), Obadiah sheltering prophets (1KI 18:3-4), Nehemiah (NEH 5:15), Hanani (NEH 7:2), Job (JOB 1:8), David (PSA 5:7; 119:38), Jonah (JON 1:9), Cornelius (ACT 10:2)

topics.name: WORSHIP
topics.subtopic: TO BE RENDERED ONLY TO GOD
topics.entry_text: WORSHIP — To be rendered only to God: EXO 20:3; DEU 5:7; 6:13; MAT 4:10; LUK 4:8; ACT 10:26; 14:15; COL 2:18; REV 19:10; 22:8

verses.heading: The First Angel's Message — Fear God as the First Imperative of the Everlasting Gospel
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: The first of three angels proclaiming messages to the whole earth in Rev 14. This angel carries the everlasting gospel and delivers three imperatives: fear God, give glory, worship the Creator. The legon participle (present active, V-PAP-NSM) connects this to v.6's gospel — these commands ARE the gospel content.
verses.notes: Phobethete (V-AOM-2P) is aorist passive imperative — a decisive, whole-action command to fear. Dote (V-2AAM-2P) is aorist active imperative. Proskynesate (V-AAM-2P) is aorist active imperative. Three aorist imperatives forming a climactic triad. Elthen (V-2AAI-3S) is aorist indicative — the judgment hour 'is come' uses aorist, possibly prophetic/proleptic aorist expressing certainty of fulfillment. Krisis (G2920) links to 1 John 4:17's 'day of judgment.'

verses.heading: The Third Angel's Warning — Beast Worship Brings Wrath
verses.kjv_text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive [his] mark in his forehead, or in his hand, The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb:
verses.context: The contrast to the first angel: those who worship the beast instead of fearing God receive divine wrath. The binary is explicit — fear God (14:7) or worship the beast (14:9).
verses.notes: Proskyneo (G4352) appears in both verses — worship God (14:7) vs. worship the beast (14:9). Same verb, opposite objects. The choice is fundamentally about whom one fears/worships.

verses.heading: The Result of the Three Angels — Commandment-Keeping and Faith
verses.kjv_text: Here is the patience of the saints: here [are] they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: The outcome description after all three angels' messages. Those who respond correctly to the messages keep God's commandments and maintain faith in Jesus.
verses.notes: Structural parallel to Eccl 12:13: fear God + keep commandments. Rev 14:7 = fear God; Rev 14:12 = keep commandments + faith of Jesus. The fear-obedience connection is the same pattern Solomon articulated.

verses.heading: Beast Worship Motivated by Fear of Power
verses.kjv_text: And they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast, saying, Who [is] like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?
verses.context: The world's worship of the beast is motivated by its apparent invincibility — 'who is able to make war with him?' This is worship based on fear of power, not love of character.
verses.notes: The rhetorical questions reveal worship motivated by intimidation. Contrast with Rev 15:4 'Who shall not fear thee, O Lord?' — the same structure but directed at God based on holiness, not raw power.

verses.heading: The Beast's Death Threat Against Those Who Refuse Worship
verses.kjv_text: And he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak, and cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed.
verses.context: The second beast enforces worship through a death threat. Those who refuse to worship the image face execution.
verses.notes: The mechanism of beast-worship is fear of death. The first angel's message counteracts this by establishing a greater fear — fear of God who can destroy both soul and body (Matt 10:28). Daniel 3:16-18 is the OT paradigm: the three Hebrews refused the death-penalty worship command.

verses.heading: Context for 4:18 — God Is Love, Boldness in the Day of Judgment
verses.kjv_text: And we have known and believed the love that God hath to us. God is love; and he that dwelleth in love dwelleth in God, and God in him. Herein is our love made perfect, that we may have boldness in the day of judgment: because as he is, so are we in this world.
verses.context: The immediate context before the 'perfect love casts out fear' statement. The purpose of perfected love is boldness (parrhesia, G3954) in the day of judgment (krisis, G2920). The fear being addressed is JUDICIAL fear — dread of the judgment day.
verses.notes: Teteliōtai (V-RPI-3S) perfect passive indicative — love has been perfected, completed state. Parrhesian (G3954 N-ASF) — boldness/confidence, the opposite of judicial fear. Kriseos (G2920 N-GSF) — the same word as Rev 14:7's 'hour of his judgment.' The context is specifically about standing confidently before God's judgment, not about eliminating all reverent awe.

verses.heading: Perfect Love Casts Out Fear — What Kind of Fear?
verses.kjv_text: There is no fear in love; but perfect love casteth out fear: because fear hath torment. He that feareth is not made perfect in love.
verses.context: John's statement about fear and love, following the judgment-day context of v.17. The specific construction kolasin echei ('has punishment/torment') defines the kind of fear — fear that anticipates punitive consequences.
verses.notes: CRITICAL PARSING: phobos (G5401 N-NSM) — generic fear. teleia agape (G5046 A-NSF + G26 N-NSF) — perfect/complete love. ballei exo (G906 V-PAI-3S + G1854 ADV) — present active indicative, continuously casts out. kolasin (G2851 N-ASF) echei (G2192 V-PAI-3S) — 'fear has punishment' — the fear itself contains/involves punishment. The construction is kolasin echei NOT meta kolaseos — fear possesses punishment as an inherent quality, not just accompaniment. phobomenos (G5399 V-PNP-NSM) — present middle/passive participle, the one who fears. teteliōtai (G5048 V-RPI-3S) — perfect passive indicative, has NOT been perfected. Kolasis (G2851) occurs only 2x in NT (here and Matt 25:46 'everlasting punishment'). The fear cast out is specifically the dread of divine punishment — NOT the reverential awe commanded in Rev 14:7.

verses.heading: Solomon's Conclusion — The OT Template for the First Angel's Message
verses.kjv_text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this [is] the whole [duty] of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether [it be] good, or whether [it be] evil.
verses.context: Solomon's final summary after examining all of life under the sun. The 'conclusion of the whole matter' distills human duty to two commands: fear God and keep commandments, grounded in the reality of judgment.

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'end'), davar (H1697 Noun.ms.Abs 'word'), hakkol (H3605 Noun.ms.Abs 'whole'), nishma (H8085 Verb.Niphal.Ptcp 'hear'), et (H853 Prep '<obj marker>'), ha'elohim (H430 Noun.mp.Abs 'God'), yera (H3372 Verb.Qal.Impv.2ms 'fear'), ve'et (waw + H853 Prep), mitsvotav (H4687 Noun.fp.Abs.+3ms 'commandments'), shemor (H8104 Verb.Qal.Impv.2ms 'keep'), ki (H3588 Conj 'for'), zeh (H2088 DemPron.ms 'this'), kol (H3605 Noun.ms.Cst 'whole'), ha'adam (H120 Noun.ms.Abs 'man')

verses.notes: HEBREW PARSING: yera (H3372 Verb.Qal.Impv.2ms) — imperative, direct command to fear. mitsvotav (H4687 Noun.fp.Abs.+3ms) — his commandments. shemor (H8104 Verb.Qal.Impv.2ms) — imperative, direct command to keep. STRUCTURAL PARALLEL TO REV 14:7,12: Fear God (Eccl 12:13 yare / Rev 14:7 phobethete) + Keep commandments (Eccl 12:13 shamar mitsvotav / Rev 14:12 terountes tas entolas tou theou) + Judgment (Eccl 12:14 mishpat / Rev 14:7 krisis). LXX bridges confirmed: yare→phobeo (241 co-occ), mitsvah→entole (153 co-occ).

verses.heading: Moses' Summary — The Five-Part Fear-Love-Serve-Obey Pattern
verses.kjv_text: And now, Israel, what doth the LORD thy God require of thee, but to fear the LORD thy God, to walk in all his ways, and to love him, and to serve the LORD thy God with all thy heart and with all thy soul, To keep the commandments of the LORD, and his statutes, which I command thee this day for thy good?
verses.context: Moses' summary of what God requires — a five-part sequence where fear comes FIRST. This is a farewell address, like Solomon's (Eccl 12:13), Joshua's (Josh 24:14), and Samuel's (1 Sam 12:14,24).
verses.notes: HEBREW PARSING — five infinitives in sequence: (1) yir'ah (H3372 Qal InfCon) — to fear; (2) laleket (H1980 Qal InfCon) — to walk; (3) ahabah (H157 Qal InfCon) — to love; (4) la'abod (H5647 Qal InfCon) — to serve; (5) lishmor (H8104 Qal InfCon, v.13) — to keep. Fear is FIRST in the sequence — not as an alternative to love but as its foundation. The same God-requires question appears in Micah 6:8.

verses.heading: The Greatest Commandment — Love God with All
verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt love the LORD thy God with all thine heart, and with all thy soul, and with all thy might.
verses.context: The Shema — the greatest commandment, quoted by Jesus in Matt 22:37. This love command sits in the SAME chapter as the fear command (6:13), demonstrating they are complementary, not contradictory.
verses.notes: Ahab (H157) — the same verb that appears in Deut 10:12's sequence alongside yare. The chapter contains BOTH love (6:5) and fear (6:13) as co-requirements of the covenant. Jesus quotes 6:5 as the greatest commandment (Matt 22:37) and quotes 6:13 during the temptation (Matt 4:10).

verses.heading: Fear, Serve, and Swear by God's Name — Exclusive Devotion
verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God, and serve him, and shalt swear by his name.
verses.context: The fear command within the Shema chapter. Jesus quotes this in Matt 4:10 during the temptation, connecting fear of God to exclusive worship.
verses.notes: Jesus quotes this as 'Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve' (Matt 4:10) — replacing 'fear' with 'worship' (proskyneo), confirming that fear and worship overlap semantically. Cross-testament parallel tool confirms Deut 6:13 → Rev 14:7 (0.304 hybrid score).

verses.heading: Jesus' Fear Hierarchy — Fear God, Not Those Who Kill the Body
verses.kjv_text: And fear not them which kill the body, but are not able to kill the soul: but rather fear him which is able to destroy both soul and body in hell.
verses.context: Jesus teaching his disciples about whom to fear. Two contrasting fears: fear of persecutors (who can only kill the body) vs. fear of God (who has power over both soul and body).
verses.notes: BOTH uses of phobeo are present middle/passive imperative (V-PNM-2P): me phobeisthe (stop fearing humans) and phobeisthe (keep fearing God). Present tense = ongoing orientation. This establishes the foundational principle for the beast/fear contrast: the one who fears God will not be moved by the beast's death threat (Rev 13:15). Parallel in Luke 12:4-5 adds explicit emphasis: 'I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear.'

verses.heading: Sinai Theophany — Two Types of Fear Distinguished in One Passage
verses.kjv_text: And all the people saw the thunderings, and the lightnings, and the noise of the trumpet, and the mountain smoking: and when the people saw [it], they removed, and stood afar off. And they said unto Moses, Speak thou with us, and we will hear: but let not God speak with us, lest we die. And Moses said unto the people, Fear not: for God is come to prove you, and that his fear may be before your faces, that ye sin not.
verses.context: The Sinai theophany after the giving of the Ten Commandments. The people respond with terror and retreat. Moses distinguishes two responses: the terror they should stop (al-tir'u) and the fear they should maintain (yir'ato).

hebrew_parsing.gloss: 'seeing'), vayyar' (H7200 Verb.Qal.Wayq.3ms 'and they saw' — NOTE: this is ra'ah H7200 'to see', NOT yare H3372 'to fear'), vayyanu'u (H5128 Verb.Qal.Wayq.3mp 'and they trembled'), vayya'amdu (H5975 Verb.Qal.Wayq.3mp 'and they stood'), merachoq (H7350 Adj.ms 'afar off')

verses.notes: CRITICAL DUAL-FEAR PASSAGE. v.18: wayyar' (H7200 ra'ah, Qal.Wayq.3ms) — 'saw,' NOT yare. v.20: al-tir'u (H3372 yare, Qal.Impf.2mp with al negation) — 'do not fear' = immediate prohibition, stop the terror. v.20: yir'ato (H3374 yirah, Noun.fs.+3ms) — 'his fear' = the moral orientation that should remain. Moses tells them to STOP fearing (terror) so that they CAN fear (reverence). Same root (y-r-'), two different senses, one verse. This is the paradigm case for the filial/slavish distinction.

verses.heading: Fear of the LORD as the Beginning of Knowledge
verses.kjv_text: The fear of the LORD [is] the beginning of knowledge: [but] fools despise wisdom and instruction.
verses.context: The programmatic statement of Proverbs, establishing yirah as the epistemological foundation — the starting point of all true knowledge.
verses.notes: Yir'at (H3374, construct form) — the fear OF the LORD. Re'shit (H7225) — beginning/chief part/first principle. The fear-wisdom connection spans Prov 1:7, 9:10, 15:33, Job 28:28, Ps 111:10, and Eccl 12:13.

verses.heading: The Fear of the LORD Defined — To Hate Evil
verses.kjv_text: The fear of the LORD [is] to hate evil: pride, and arrogancy, and the evil way, and the froward mouth, do I hate.
verses.context: Wisdom personified defines the fear of the LORD with active moral content — not passive emotion but active moral opposition to evil.
verses.notes: Yir'at YHWH IS to hate evil — equational sentence. Fear is not a feeling but a moral orientation. The list (pride, arrogancy, evil way, froward mouth) specifies what fear-of-God opposes.

verses.heading: Fear of the LORD as the Beginning of Wisdom
verses.kjv_text: The fear of the LORD [is] the beginning of wisdom: and the knowledge of the holy [is] understanding.
verses.context: Parallel to Prov 1:7 — bookends the first major section of Proverbs.
verses.notes: Chokmah (H2451) — wisdom, paired with yirah as co-beginnings. The construct phrase 'fear of the LORD' (yir'at YHWH) appears 14 times in Proverbs alone.

verses.heading: Fear of the LORD Produces Confidence and Life, Not Terror
verses.kjv_text: In the fear of the LORD [is] strong confidence: and his children shall have a place of refuge. The fear of the LORD [is] a fountain of life, to depart from the snares of death.
verses.context: Two verses that directly contradict the assumption that fear = terror. Fear produces 'strong confidence' (not anxiety) and is a 'fountain of life' (not death).
verses.notes: Mibtach (H4009) — confidence, security. Ma'yan (H4726) — fountain, spring. These are the FRUITS of fearing God: confidence and life. This is the opposite profile of servile fear, which produces anxiety and death-dread.

verses.heading: By the Fear of the LORD Men Depart from Evil
verses.kjv_text: By mercy and truth iniquity is purged: and by the fear of the LORD [men] depart from evil.
verses.context: Fear as moral compass — paired with mercy and truth as mechanisms of moral transformation.
verses.notes: Sur mera (H5493 + H7451) — to turn aside from evil. Fear produces moral departure from evil, not terror-induced paralysis.

verses.heading: The Messianic Endowment — Even the Messiah Operates with Godly Fear
verses.kjv_text: And the spirit of the LORD shall rest upon him, the spirit of wisdom and understanding, the spirit of counsel and might, the spirit of knowledge and of the fear of the LORD; And shall make him of quick understanding in the fear of the LORD: and he shall not judge after the sight of his eyes, neither reprove after the hearing of his ears:
verses.context: The sevenfold Spirit resting on the Messiah, culminating in 'the fear of the LORD.' If the Messiah himself delights in the fear of God, the command to fear God cannot be sub-Christian.
verses.notes: Yir'at YHWH (H3374) — the climactic seventh quality of the Messianic Spirit. V.3: 'quick understanding' (haricho) literally 'his delighting' in the fear of the LORD. Christ's relationship with the Father was characterized by godly fear (cf. Heb 5:7).

verses.heading: Abraham's Test — Fear Demonstrated Through Obedience, Not Terror
verses.kjv_text: And he said, Lay not thine hand upon the lad, neither do thou any thing unto him: for now I know that thou fearest God, seeing thou hast not withheld thy son, thine only [son] from me.
verses.context: The conclusion of the Aqedah (binding of Isaac). God declares Abraham 'fears God' based on his willingness to obey, not on an emotion of terror.
verses.notes: Yere elohim (H3373 + H430) — 'one who fears God,' adjectival form. The evidence of fear is obedience (not withholding Isaac), not emotional state. Moskala notes the wordplay: yare (fear) and ra'ah (see) at Moriah (Gen 22:14 'the LORD will see/provide').

verses.heading: Jesus' Explicit Fear-of-God Teaching — 'I Will Forewarn You Whom to Fear'
verses.kjv_text: And I say unto you my friends, Be not afraid of them that kill the body, and after that have no more that they can do. But I will forewarn you whom ye shall fear: Fear him, which after he hath killed hath power to cast into hell; yea, I say unto you, Fear him.
verses.context: Luke's parallel to Matt 10:28 with added emphasis. Jesus personally directs ('I will forewarn you') whom to fear, and repeats the command twice.
verses.notes: The doubled 'Fear him' intensifies the command. Jesus is not eliminating fear but redirecting it — from human persecutors to God. This is the same logic as the first angel's message.

verses.heading: Spirit of Adoption, Not Bondage to Fear — Two Types of Spiritual Relationship
verses.kjv_text: For ye have not received the spirit of bondage again to fear; but ye have received the Spirit of adoption, whereby we cry, Abba, Father.
verses.context: Paul contrasts two spiritual conditions: bondage (douleia) producing fear (phobos) vs. adoption (huiothesia) producing intimacy ('Abba, Father').
verses.notes: Pneuma douleias eis phobon (G1397 + G5401) — spirit of slavery leading to fear. Pneuma huiothesias (G5206) — spirit of adoption/sonship. The fear ELIMINATED is the slave's dread of a master, not the child's reverence for a father. The adoption metaphor confirms the filial/slavish distinction: adopted children don't cower in terror but call out 'Abba!' — yet they still reverence their Father (1 Pet 1:17).

verses.heading: God Has Not Given a Spirit of Cowardice
verses.kjv_text: For God hath not given us the spirit of fear; but of power, and of love, and of a sound mind.
verses.context: Paul's encouragement to Timothy. The 'fear' here is NOT phobos but deilia (G1167) — cowardice/timidity.
verses.notes: Deilia (G1167), NOT phobos (G5401). Deilia is always negative — cowardice, faithlessness. Paul denies God gives deilia (cowardice) while affirming power, love, and a sound mind. This is not a denial of reverential fear but a denial of cowardly timidity. Rev 21:8 lists 'the fearful' (deiloi, G1169 — same root) first among the lost.

verses.heading: Serve God with Reverence and Godly Fear — Grace and Fear Coexist
verses.kjv_text: Wherefore we receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved, let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear: For our God [is] a consuming fire.
verses.context: The conclusion of the Hebrews author's argument about the superiority of the new covenant. Even under grace, believers are to serve with eulabeia (reverent caution) and deos (awe).
verses.notes: Eulabeias (G2124 N-GSF) — reverent caution, godly fear. Deous (listed as G1169 in parser but actually deos G127a) — awe/reverence. These words appear in the context of RECEIVING GRACE (charin echomen) — grace does not eliminate reverent fear but provides the ground for it. 'Our God is a consuming fire' (v.29, quoting Deut 4:24) reinforces that reverent fear remains appropriate.

verses.heading: Call God 'Father' AND Pass Time in Fear — Both Coexist
verses.kjv_text: And if ye call on the Father, who without respect of persons judgeth according to every man's work, pass the time of your sojourning [here] in fear:
verses.context: Peter tells believers who call God 'Father' to live 'in fear' (en phobo). Calling God Father and fearing God coexist in the same sentence, from the same apostolic tradition as 1 John.
verses.notes: En phobo (G5401) — in fear. The same writer-tradition that produced 1 John 4:18 sees no contradiction between filial relationship ('Father') and reverential fear. This is the clearest proof that 1 John 4:18 does not eliminate all fear.

verses.heading: Cornelius — Practical Example of Fearing God
verses.kjv_text: [A] devout [man], and one that feared God with all his house, which gave much alms to the people, and prayed to God alway.
verses.context: The description of Cornelius before Peter's visit. The practical manifestation of fearing God: devotion, generosity, prayer, and righteousness.
verses.notes: Phoboumenos ton theon (G5399, present middle/passive participle) — 'one who fears God.' Cornelius demonstrates what fearing God looks like in practice: devout living, generous giving, constant prayer. Acts 10:35 adds: 'in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted.'

verses.heading: Forgiveness Exists So That God May Be Feared
verses.kjv_text: But there is forgiveness with thee, that thou mayest be feared.
verses.context: A De Profundis psalm — out of the depths, the psalmist discovers that forgiveness PRODUCES fear rather than eliminating it.
verses.notes: HEBREW: ha-selichah (H5547 Noun.fs — forgiveness) lema'an (purpose conjunction — 'so that') tivvare (H3372 Niphal.Impf.2ms — 'you may be feared'). PURPOSE CLAUSE: forgiveness exists FOR THE PURPOSE of producing the fear of God. This demolishes the false dichotomy between grace and fear. The experience of being forgiven produces reverent awe — understanding the depth of sin and the magnitude of mercy. Cross-testament parallel tool confirms 1 John 4:18 → Ps 130:4 (0.354 hybrid score).

verses.heading: The Three Hebrews — Fear of God Overrides Fear of the Death Threat
verses.kjv_text: Shadrach, Meshach, and Abednego, answered and said to the king, O Nebuchadnezzar, we [are] not careful to answer thee in this matter. If it be [so], our God whom we serve is able to deliver us from the burning fiery furnace, and he will deliver [us] out of thine hand, O king. But if not, be it known unto thee, O king, that we will not serve thy gods, nor worship the golden image which thou hast set up.
verses.context: The paradigmatic OT case of fearing God over fearing a death threat. Nebuchadnezzar's command mirrors Rev 13:15 — worship the image or die.
verses.notes: The five-fold pattern (manufactured image, universal worship command, death penalty, faithful refusal, divine vindication) parallels Revelation 13. 'But if not' (v.18) shows their refusal is unconditional — not dependent on deliverance. Their fear of God exceeds their fear of the furnace. This is the existential logic of the first angel's message.

verses.heading: Jonah's Confession — Miniature Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, I [am] an Hebrew; and I fear the LORD, the God of heaven, which hath made the sea and the dry [land].
verses.context: Jonah's confession to the pagan sailors contains every element of Rev 14:7: fear, the LORD, God of heaven, Creator of sea and land.
verses.notes: Cross-testament parallel tool confirms Jonah 1:9 → Rev 14:7 (0.383 hybrid score with keywords fear, heaven, sea). The structural parallel is remarkable: 'I fear the LORD, the God of heaven, which hath made the sea' maps directly onto 'Fear God... worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea.'

verses.heading: False Fear — Taught by the Precept of Men
verses.kjv_text: Wherefore the Lord said, Forasmuch as this people draw near [me] with their mouth, and with their lips do honour me, but have removed their heart far from me, and their fear toward me is taught by the precept of men:
verses.context: God's indictment of Israel's worship. Their fear (yirah) is misdirected — taught by human tradition rather than grounded in God's character. Jesus quotes this in Matt 15:8-9.
verses.notes: Yir'atam (H3374 + 3mp suffix) — 'their fear toward me.' The problem is not the absence of fear but its corruption: it's 'taught by the precept of men' rather than arising from genuine knowledge of God. Jesus' quotation (Matt 15:9) links this to 'vain worship' — when fear is wrong, worship becomes wrong. This illuminates the fear → worship connection in Rev 14:7.

verses.heading: Jesus Quotes Deut 6:13 — Worship, Fear, and Service Unified
verses.kjv_text: Then saith Jesus unto him, Get thee hence, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve.
verses.context: Jesus' final response to Satan's temptation, quoting Deut 6:13. Notably, Jesus replaces 'fear' (yare) with 'worship' (proskyneo), treating them as interchangeable in context.
verses.notes: Proskyneseis (G4352) — worship. The substitution of proskyneo for yare confirms the semantic overlap: to fear God IS to worship God. The same verb proskyneo appears in Rev 14:7's third imperative.

verses.heading: Those Who Fear the LORD — Community and Divine Remembrance
verses.kjv_text: Then they that feared the LORD spake often one to another: and the LORD hearkened, and heard [it], and a book of remembrance was written before him for them that feared the LORD, and that thought upon his name.
verses.context: God-fearers form community, speak to one another, and God takes notice — a book of remembrance is written.
verses.notes: Fear of God produces community (spake often one to another) and divine attention (a book of remembrance). This is the positive fruit, not the terror profile.

verses.heading: The Most Complete OT Parallel to Rev 14:6-7
verses.kjv_text: For the LORD [is] great, and greatly to be praised: he [is] to be feared above all gods. For all the gods of the nations [are] idols: but the LORD made the heavens. Honour and majesty [are] before him: strength and beauty [are] in his sanctuary. Give unto the LORD, O ye kindreds of the people, give unto the LORD glory and strength. Give unto the LORD the glory [due unto] his name: bring an offering, and come into his courts. O worship the LORD in the beauty of holiness: fear before him, all the earth.
verses.context: This psalm contains every element of Rev 14:7: fear, glory, worship, Creator, universal scope ('all the earth'). Ps 96:13 adds judgment.
verses.notes: Feared (nora, H3372 Niphal participle), give glory (kavod, H3519), worship (hishtachavu, H7812), Creator (made the heavens), universal scope (all the earth). This is the fullest single-passage OT parallel to the first angel's message.

verses.heading: Eschatological Praise — Fear and Glory United
verses.kjv_text: Who shall not fear thee, O Lord, and glorify thy name? for [thou] only [art] holy: for all nations shall come and worship before thee; for thy judgments are made manifest.
verses.context: The song of the victorious saints after the beast. Fear, glory, worship, and judgment all appear — echoing Rev 14:7 in the context of victory.
verses.notes: The rhetorical question 'Who shall not fear thee?' expects the answer 'No one' — universal fear of God is the eschatological goal. Fear + glorify + worship + judgments = the complete pattern of Rev 14:7. Even in final victory, the saints fear God.

verses.heading: Make God Your Fear and Your Dread — Not the Human Threat
verses.kjv_text: Sanctify the LORD of hosts himself; and [let] him [be] your fear, and [let] him [be] your dread.
verses.context: Isaiah's command to redirect fear from the Syro-Ephraimite coalition (8:12) to God himself.
verses.notes: Mora (H4172) — 'your fear/dread object.' God should BE the object of fear. Same logic as Matt 10:28 and the first angel's message: redirect fear from human threats to God.

verses.heading: Demonic Fear — Believing and Trembling Without Saving Response
verses.kjv_text: Thou believest that there is one God; thou doest well: the devils also believe, and tremble.
verses.context: James distinguishes intellectual belief (which even demons have) from saving faith. Demons tremble (phrissousin) but do not submit.
verses.notes: Phrissousin (G5425) — to bristle, shudder. NOT phobeo or phobos. Demonic fear is raw terror without submission — they believe and tremble but do not obey. This is a third category beyond filial and slavish: existential terror with no relational orientation.

verses.heading: Work Out Salvation with Fear and Trembling
verses.kjv_text: Wherefore, my beloved, as ye have always obeyed, not as in my presence only, but now much more in my absence, work out your own salvation with fear and trembling.
verses.context: Paul commands believers to pursue sanctification 'with fear and trembling' — fear as a component of the Christian life, not eliminated by grace.
verses.notes: Meta phobou kai tromou (G5401 + G5156) — 'with fear and trembling.' Paul uses this phrase in multiple contexts (1 Cor 2:3, Eph 6:5). Fear and trembling characterize the SANCTIFIED life, not the pre-conversion life only.

verses.heading: Fear of Judgment as Evangelistic Motivation
verses.kjv_text: For we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ; that every one may receive the things [done] in [his] body, according to that he hath done, whether [it be] good or bad. Knowing therefore the terror of the Lord, we persuade men; but we are made manifest unto God; and I trust also are made manifest in your consciences.
verses.context: Paul connects the reality of judgment to evangelistic motivation. 'Knowing the terror of the Lord' is the basis for persuading others.
verses.notes: Ton phobon tou kyriou (G5401) — 'the fear/terror of the Lord.' Paul, the apostle of grace, uses the fear of judgment as motivation for gospel ministry. This directly parallels the first angel's logic: judgment is coming (Rev 14:7), therefore fear God.

verses.heading: Jesus Himself Operated with Godly Fear
verses.kjv_text: Who in the days of his flesh, when he had offered up prayers and supplications with strong crying and tears unto him that was able to save him from death, and was heard in that he feared;
verses.context: The author of Hebrews describes Jesus' prayer life, noting he 'was heard in that he feared' (eulabeia, G2124).
verses.notes: Eulabeias (G2124) — reverent caution, godly fear. If Jesus himself operated with eulabeia, the command to fear God is not sub-Christian but Christ-like. The same word appears in Heb 12:28 for the fear believers should have.

verses.heading: Noah Moved with Fear — Reverent Caution as Faith in Action
verses.kjv_text: By faith Noah, being warned of God of things not seen as yet, moved with fear, prepared an ark to the saving of his house; by the which he condemned the world, and became heir of the righteousness which is by faith.
verses.context: Noah's faith-response to the flood warning. His 'fear' (eulabeomai, G2125) is not terror but reverent caution that produces constructive action.
verses.notes: Eulabetheis (G2125, aorist passive participle) — 'having been moved with reverent caution.' This is faith's fear-component: taking God's warning seriously enough to act on it. Fear and faith are not opposed — they work together.

verses.heading: John's Own Definition — Love IS Keeping Commandments
verses.kjv_text: For this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments: and his commandments are not grievous.
verses.context: The same John who wrote 'perfect love casts out fear' defines love as keeping commandments — the same pairing as Eccl 12:13 and Rev 14:7,12.
verses.notes: Haute estin he agape tou theou (G26) — 'this IS the love of God.' John equates love with obedience. If love = commandment-keeping, and fear → commandment-keeping (Eccl 12:13, Deut 10:12-13), then fear and love are complementary paths to the same result.

verses.heading: Fear Connected to Sabbath-Keeping
verses.kjv_text: Ye shall fear every man his mother, and his father, and keep my sabbaths: I [am] the LORD your God.
verses.context: Fear of parents directly linked to Sabbath observance in the Holiness Code.
verses.notes: Fear (yare H3372) + keep sabbaths (shamar H8104 + shabbat H7676). Fear and Sabbath-keeping linked in one command — relevant to the Rev 14:7,12 connection where fear of God and commandment-keeping are paired.

verses.heading: The Fearful (Cowardly) Among the Lost
verses.kjv_text: But the fearful, and unbelieving, and the abominable, and murderers, and whoremongers, and sorcerers, and idolaters, and all liars, shall have their part in the lake which burneth with fire and brimstone: which is the second death.
verses.context: The final vice list of Revelation. 'The fearful' (deiloi, G1169) heads the list — cowardly retreat from faith is a damning failure.
verses.notes: Deilois (G1169) — cowardly, timid, faithless. NOT phobos. The cowardly are those who fear persecution/death more than God. They are the opposite of those who fear God (Rev 14:7). The beast-worship scenario (Rev 13:15) is exactly the situation that separates the phobeo-fearers from the deilos-cowards.

verses.heading: The First Imperative — Fear God (Standalone)
verses.kjv_text: Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: The central verse of the study — the first angel's three imperatives: fear, give glory, worship. Grounded in the judgment hour and Creator identity.
verses.notes: Phobethete (G5399 V-AOM-2P aorist passive imperative) — decisive whole-action command. Dote (G1325 V-2AAM-2P aorist active imperative). Proskynesate (G4352 V-AAM-2P aorist active imperative). Elthen (G2064 V-2AAI-3S aorist indicative) — possibly prophetic/proleptic aorist. Kriseos (G2920 N-GSF) — judgment.

verses.heading: Fearing God and Working Righteousness — Accepted in Every Nation
verses.kjv_text: But in every nation he that feareth him, and worketh righteousness, is accepted with him.
verses.context: Peter's conclusion after Cornelius's vision — fearing God is the universal criterion for acceptance, transcending national boundaries.
verses.notes: Phoboumenos auton (G5399 present middle/passive participle) — 'the one who fears him.' Fear of God paired with working righteousness as the dual marker of those accepted by God. Universal scope ('every nation') parallels Rev 14:6's 'every nation, kindred, tongue, and people.'

verses.heading: Fear of the LORD as Beginning of Wisdom — Psalmic Echo of Proverbs
verses.kjv_text: The fear of the LORD [is] the beginning of wisdom: a good understanding have all they that do [his commandments]: his praise endureth for ever.
verses.context: A wisdom psalm echoing Prov 1:7 and 9:10. Adds that those who DO his commandments have good understanding — linking fear to obedience.
verses.notes: Yir'at YHWH (H3374) re'shit chokmah (H2451). Adds the obedience element: 'all they that do his commandments' — the same fear-obedience pairing as Eccl 12:13 and Rev 14:7,12.

verses.heading: Fear the LORD — Taught and Cultivated, Not Merely Felt
verses.kjv_text: O fear the LORD, ye his saints: for [there is] no want to them that fear him. The young lions do lack, and suffer hunger: but they that seek the LORD shall not want any good [thing]. Come, ye children, hearken unto me: I will teach you the fear of the LORD.
verses.context: David invites believers to fear the LORD, promises provision for those who do, and offers to TEACH the fear of the LORD — indicating it is a learnable disposition, not merely an emotion.
verses.notes: V.11: 'I will teach you the fear of the LORD' — yirah is something TAUGHT and cultivated, not just spontaneously felt. This is significant: if fear were mere terror, it could not be taught. The pedagogical framing confirms that fear-of-God is a moral/relational orientation that can be developed.

verses.heading: God's Desire — That They Would Fear Him and Keep Commandments
verses.kjv_text: O that there were such an heart in them, that they would fear me, and keep all my commandments always, that it might be well with them, and with their children for ever!
verses.context: God's own expressed desire after the people heard the Ten Commandments. God WANTS his people to fear him — it is not a punishment but a blessing.
verses.notes: Yire (H3372 Qal Impf) — 'that they would fear.' God expresses this as a WISH — he desires their fear. The pairing with 'keep all my commandments' echoes the Eccl 12:13 pattern. The purpose clause 'that it might be well with them' shows fear is FOR THEIR GOOD.

verses.heading: God Demands the Fear Due a Master and Father
verses.kjv_text: A son honoureth [his] father, and a servant his master: if then I [be] a father, where [is] mine honour? and if I [be] a master, where [is] my fear? saith the LORD of hosts unto you, O priests, that despise my name. And ye say, Wherein have we despised thy name?
verses.context: God complains that the priests withhold the mora (fear/reverence) that is his due. Both the father-son and master-servant relationships require appropriate fear.
verses.notes: Mora'i (H4172 + 1cs suffix) — 'my fear/reverence.' God claims the right to be feared as both Father and Master. The father-son metaphor is significant: filial fear (as a son honors a father) is exactly what God demands. This is not slavish terror but family reverence.

verses.heading: Fear of God Protects the Vulnerable
verses.kjv_text: Thou shalt not curse the deaf, nor put a stumblingblock before the blind, but shalt fear thy God: I [am] the LORD.
verses.context: Fear of God as the ethical foundation for protecting vulnerable people — the deaf and blind who cannot protect themselves.
verses.notes: Yare (H3372) elohekha — 'fear thy God.' Fear of God functions as the moral restraint that prevents exploitation of the defenseless. When no human witness can hold you accountable, the fear of God does.

verses.heading: Fear of God Prevents Economic Oppression
verses.kjv_text: Ye shall not therefore oppress one another; but thou shalt fear thy God: for I [am] the LORD your God.
verses.context: In the Jubilee legislation, fear of God is the motive for just economic relations — preventing oppression in business dealings.
verses.notes: Yare (H3372) elohekha — 'fear thy God.' Fear of God as the basis for economic justice. The 'I am the LORD' formula reinforces that God watches over transactions where exploitation might otherwise go unchecked.

verses.heading: Nehemiah's Fear of God Contrasted with Previous Governors' Oppression
verses.kjv_text: But the former governors that [had been] before me were chargeable unto the people, and had taken of them bread and wine, beside forty shekels of silver; yea, even their servants bare rule over the people: but so did not I, because of the fear of God.
verses.context: Nehemiah's personal testimony — his fear of God prevented him from exploiting his position of power, unlike previous governors.
verses.notes: Yir'at elohim (H3374 + H430) — 'the fear of God.' A practical example of fear-of-God as moral compass in governance. Fear of God produces JUSTICE, not terror.

verses.heading: The Fear of the Lord IS Wisdom — Conclusion of the Wisdom Poem
verses.kjv_text: And unto man he said, Behold, the fear of the Lord, that [is] wisdom; and to depart from evil [is] understanding.
verses.context: The climax of Job's wisdom poem (ch. 28). After asking 'Where shall wisdom be found?' the answer is: fear of the Lord IS wisdom.
verses.notes: Yir'at Adonai (H3374 + H136) — 'the fear of the Lord.' Equational: fear IS wisdom. Parallels Prov 1:7, 9:10, Ps 111:10. Paired with 'depart from evil' = the same moral-compass function as Prov 8:13.

verses.heading: Everlasting Punishment — The Second Occurrence of Kolasis
verses.kjv_text: And these shall go away into everlasting punishment: but the righteous into life eternal.
verses.context: The conclusion of the sheep-and-goats judgment parable. Contains the other NT occurrence of kolasis (G2851) — 'everlasting punishment.'
verses.notes: Kolasin aionion (G2851 + G166) — 'everlasting punishment.' The only other NT use of kolasis besides 1 John 4:18. The judicial/eschatological context confirms that kolasis belongs to the punishment/judgment semantic field — supporting the reading that 1 John 4:18's 'fear has kolasis' means fear of divine judicial consequences.

verses.heading: God Acts So That Men Should Fear Before Him
verses.kjv_text: I know that, whatsoever God doeth, it shall be for ever: nothing can be put to it, nor any thing taken from it: and God doeth [it], that [men] should fear before him.
verses.context: Solomon observes that God's works are eternal and unchangeable, and the PURPOSE of divine action is to produce fear.
verses.notes: Yir'u (H3372 Qal Impf 3mp) millepanav — 'that they should fear before him.' Purpose clause: God acts SO THAT people fear him. Fear is the intended response to encountering God's sovereign works.

verses.heading: It Shall Be Well with Those Who Fear God
verses.kjv_text: Though a sinner do evil an hundred times, and his [days] be prolonged, yet surely I know that it shall be well with them that fear God, which fear before him: But it shall not be well with the wicked, neither shall he prolong [his] days, [which are] as a shadow; because he feareth not before God.
verses.context: Solomon's contrast: those who fear God will ultimately prosper; the wicked who do not fear will not. Fear distinguishes the righteous from the wicked.
verses.notes: Yere (H3372) ha-elohim — 'those who fear God.' The binary is clear: fearing God leads to well-being; not fearing God leads to ruin. This is the same binary as Rev 14:7 (fear God) vs. Rev 14:9-10 (worship the beast → wrath).

verses.heading: Perfecting Holiness in the Fear of God
verses.kjv_text: Having therefore these promises, dearly beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from all filthiness of the flesh and spirit, perfecting holiness in the fear of God.
verses.context: Paul grounds the pursuit of holiness in 'the fear of God' — fear as the environment for sanctification, not an impediment to it.
verses.notes: En phobo theou (G5401 + G2316) — 'in the fear of God.' Paul the apostle of grace commands sanctification IN the fear of God. Cross-testament parallel tool confirms 2 Cor 7:1 → Deut 6:13 (0.323 hybrid score with 'covenant, fear'). Fear and grace coexist in Pauline theology.

verses.heading: Reward for Those Who Fear God's Name
verses.kjv_text: And the nations were angry, and thy wrath is come, and the time of the dead, that they should be judged, and that thou shouldest give reward unto thy servants the prophets, and to the saints, and them that fear thy name, small and great; and shouldest destroy them which destroy the earth.
verses.context: The seventh trumpet announcement — judgment comes, and those who 'fear thy name' receive reward alongside the prophets and saints.
verses.notes: Tous phoboumenous to onoma sou (G5399 present middle/passive participle) — 'those who fear thy name.' Fear of God as the characteristic of the redeemed at the final judgment. Small and great — universal scope matching Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Final Praise — All Who Fear God Called to Worship
verses.kjv_text: And a voice came out of the throne, saying, Praise our God, all ye his servants, and ye that fear him, both small and great.
verses.context: A voice from the throne calls God's servants and those who fear him to praise — fear and praise coexist in the final worship scene.
verses.notes: Hoi phoboumenoi auton (G5399 present middle/passive participle) — 'those who fear him.' Even in the final praise scene, God-fearers are identified as a distinct group called to worship. Fear persists in the redeemed community.

verses.heading: Serve the LORD with Fear and Rejoice with Trembling
verses.kjv_text: Serve the LORD with fear, and rejoice with trembling.
verses.context: A royal/messianic psalm commanding both fear and joy simultaneously — they are not mutually exclusive.
verses.notes: Yirah (H3374) + gilu (H1524, rejoicing) — fear and rejoicing coexist in the same command. This directly contradicts the assumption that fear eliminates joy. Serve with fear AND rejoice — the two are complementary, not contradictory.

word_studies.summary: Primary OT verb for 'fear' — covers the full spectrum from terror (Gen 3:10) to reverence (Eccl 12:13). Qal dominates (282 of 332 forms) covering both fear and awe. Niphal participle 'nora' = awesome/terrible, applied to God. Collocates with YHWH 130x (41%) forming the 'fear the LORD' covenant formula. Deuteronomy has highest raw count (35). LXX maps to phobeo (G5399) with 241 co-occurrences (PMI 5.75).
word_studies.relevance: The ROOT behind the entire yare/phobos tradition. Rev 14:7's phobethete inherits this full semantic range through the LXX bridge. The dual use in Exod 20:18-20 (stop terror / maintain reverence) is the paradigm for the filial/slavish distinction.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun from yare — 'the fear of the LORD' as sustained disposition. Proverbs accounts for 14 of 42 uses. Dominant in wisdom tradition: beginning of knowledge (Prov 1:7), beginning of wisdom (Prov 9:10), hate evil (Prov 8:13). LXX maps to phobos (G5401) 31x; also to eusebeia ('godliness') 3x — LXX translators understood yirah as a life-orientation, not mere terror.
word_studies.relevance: The NOUN form that dominates the wisdom tradition. Its consistent association with wisdom, life, confidence, and moral orientation (not terror) demonstrates that the 'fear of the LORD' is a settled disposition of reverent awe.

word_studies.summary: NT verb 'to fear/revere' — the LXX equivalent of yare. 90 unique verses across 14 books. Nearly all forms middle/passive (deponent). Present imperative for ongoing fear; aorist imperative for decisive action. Rev 14:7's phobethete is aorist passive imperative — unique decisive call. Collocates with theos 15x. Spans from supernatural terror (Matt 17:6) to reverent awe (Acts 10:2) to anxious dread (Matt 25:25).
word_studies.relevance: The VERB in Rev 14:7's command. Inherits the full range of yare through 241 LXX co-occurrences. The aorist passive imperative form is a decisive, whole-action command.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun 'fear/terror/reverence.' 44 verses, 47 tokens across 16 books. Hebrew sources: pachad H6343 (35 co-occ) and yirah H3374 (31 co-occ) — inherits BOTH terror and reverence sides. Collocates with theos 9x ('fear of God'). In 1 John 4:18, phobos is cast out by perfect love — but context specifies this is fear-with-punishment (kolasin echei), not reverential awe.
word_studies.relevance: The NOUN in 1 John 4:18 — the specific fear being cast out. Its dual Hebrew source (pachad + yirah) explains the semantic ambiguity: context must determine whether terror or reverence is meant.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'punishment/torment' — only 2 NT occurrences: Matt 25:46 ('everlasting punishment') and 1 John 4:18 ('fear hath torment'). From kolazō (to curtail/punish). Distinguished from timōria (retributive penalty) — kolasis has judicial/corrective connotation. In 1 John 4:18, the construction is kolasin echei ('fear has/possesses punishment') — fear itself contains a punishing quality.
word_studies.relevance: CRITICAL for 1 John 4:18 interpretation. The rarity (only 2 NT uses) and the semantic field (judicial punishment) confirm that the fear cast out by love is specifically the dread of divine punishment — NOT reverential awe.

word_studies.summary: Adjective 'complete/perfect/mature.' From telos (end/goal). 17 verses, 19 tokens. KJV 'perfect' 88%. Semantic range: moral maturity (Matt 5:48), mature understanding (1 Cor 2:6), lacking nothing (Jas 1:4), perfect quality (1 John 4:18). In 1 John 4:18, teleia agape = love that has reached its goal/completion. Antonym: nepios (infant/immature).
word_studies.relevance: Defines what kind of love casts out fear: love that has reached its GOAL, love that is COMPLETE. Not abstract perfection but functional maturity — the love of one who knows God's character and trusts his purposes.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to worship/prostrate.' From pros (toward) + kuneo (to kiss). 54 unique verses, 65 tokens. KJV always 'worship/worshipped.' The third imperative of Rev 14:7 and the verb for beast-worship in Rev 13:4,8,12,15. Same word for both: the choice is whom to worship.
word_studies.relevance: The behavioral expression of fearing God. Rev 14:7 climaxes with proskynesate — worship the Creator. The same verb describes beast-worship in Rev 13, making the contest explicit.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to bow down/worship/prostrate.' 166 verses, 194 tokens across 25 books. Hishtaphel stem dominates (99%). KJV 'worship' 46%, 'bowed' 29%. The primary Hebrew behind proskyneo in worship contexts.
word_studies.relevance: The OT worship verb behind proskyneo. The fear → worship connection (Rev 14:7) maps onto the yare → shachah pattern in the OT.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'love' — 116 tokens in 106 verses. KJV 'love' 64%, 'charity' 18%. Strongest density in 1 John (13.3 per 100 verses). The teleia agape of 1 John 4:18 is love that has reached its goal. Antonym of phobos in 1 John 4:18.
word_studies.relevance: The love that casts out fear. John defines agape as keeping commandments (1 John 5:3) — the same obedience that flows from fearing God (Eccl 12:13). Love and fear produce the same result.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to throw/cast.' 106 verses, 127 tokens. Highest density in Revelation (5.7 per 100 verses). In 1 John 4:18, ballei exo = present active indicative 3S — love CONTINUOUSLY casts out fear. The ongoing present tense suggests a dynamic process, not a one-time event.
word_studies.relevance: The verb for love's action against fear. Present active indicative = ongoing, continuous casting out. Love doesn't eliminate fear once; it continuously displaces servile fear.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'glory/honor/splendor.' 151 verses, 168 tokens. From dokeo (to seem). Shifted from classical 'opinion' to LXX 'visible majesty' (translating kavod H3519). The second imperative of Rev 14:7: 'give glory to him.'
word_studies.relevance: The middle imperative of Rev 14:7's triad (fear → give glory → worship). Giving glory is the bridge between fearing God and worshipping him.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'judgment/decision.' 47 verses in NT. From krino (to judge). KJV 'judgment' 55%. In Rev 14:7: 'the hour of his judgment (krisis) is come.' In 1 John 4:17: 'boldness in the day of judgment (krisis).' Same word in both — linking the fear-of-God command to the judgment context.
word_studies.relevance: Connects Rev 14:7 and 1 John 4:17 — both address the 'day of judgment.' The first angel announces judgment (producing proper fear); 1 John 4:17-18 addresses judicial fear (casting out punishment-dread). Same judgment, different fear-responses.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun 'judgment/justice/ordinance.' 406 verses, 448 tokens across 31 books. From shaphat (to judge). KJV 'judgment' 72%. The Hebrew counterpart to krisis. In Eccl 12:14: 'God shall bring every work into judgment (mishpat).'
word_studies.relevance: The OT judgment word paralleling Rev 14:7's krisis. Eccl 12:14's mishpat maps to Rev 14:7's krisis through the LXX.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to keep/guard/watch.' 408 tokens. Primitive root meaning 'to hedge about with thorns.' KJV 'keep' 55%. In Eccl 12:13 and Deut 10:13: 'keep his commandments.' The obedience-verb paired with yare in the fear-obedience pattern.
word_studies.relevance: The 'keep' in 'fear God and keep his commandments.' Paired with yare in every farewell address: Moses, Joshua, Samuel, Solomon.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'terror/dread/horror.' 20 occurrences. From unused root meaning to frighten. Concentrated in Job (6 of 20). Describes visceral, involuntary terror: the horror of Abram's vision (Gen 15:12), terror falling on nations (Exod 15:16), Job's complaint about God's terrifying presence (Job 9:34, 13:21). Also used for idols as 'bugbears' (Jer 50:38). LXX maps to phobos 11x.
word_studies.relevance: DISTINCT from yirah — represents the servile terror side of fear. Job wants eymah REMOVED (9:34, 13:21). This is the type of fear that 1 John 4:18 addresses (fear with punishment). Eymah contrasts with yirah as slavish fear contrasts with filial fear.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun 'dread/sudden alarm.' 51 occurrences. From H6342 (to be startled). Concentrated in Job (11), Psalms (9). Denotes both the emotion (sudden dread) and the object feared (the dreadful thing). More visceral/physical than yare. LXX maps to phobos 35x — the LARGEST Hebrew source for phobos.
word_studies.relevance: Completes the Hebrew fear-word triad: yirah (reverence), eymah (horror), pachad (sudden dread). Pachad is the biggest LXX contributor to phobos (35 co-occ), meaning phobos inherits the alarm-side as well as the reverence-side.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to be circumspect/reverent.' Only 2 NT occurrences: Heb 11:7 (Noah 'moved with fear') and Acts 23:10 (tribune 'fearing' for Paul). From eu (well) + lambano (to take) — 'to take/handle well.' Always positive: reverent caution that produces constructive action. Distinguished from raw terror by deliberate, thoughtful response.
word_studies.relevance: Represents the POSITIVE fear category in Greek — the fear that accompanies faith (Heb 11:7). Noah's eulabeomai is the paradigm: fear + faith = constructive action (building the ark). This is the kind of fear Rev 14:7 commands.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'godly fear/reverence.' Only 2 occurrences, both in Hebrews: 5:7 (Christ 'was heard in that he feared') and 12:28 ('serve God with reverence and godly fear'). The noun form of eulabeomai. Paired with aidos (reverence/modesty) in 12:28. Grace-context: receiving an unshakable kingdom → serve with eulabeia.
word_studies.relevance: CRITICAL: Christ himself had eulabeia (Heb 5:7), and believers under grace are to serve with eulabeia (Heb 12:28). If Jesus feared God this way, the command to fear God (Rev 14:7) is not sub-Christian but Christlike.

word_studies.summary: Adjective 'cowardly/timid/fearful.' Only 3 NT occurrences: Matt 8:26 and Mark 4:40 (Jesus rebukes disciples' cowardice in the storm) and Rev 21:8 (the cowardly head the list of the lost). Always negative — linked to oligopistos (little faith) and apistos (unbelief). The deil- family (G1167 deilia, G1168 deiliao, G1169 deilos) stands in CONTRAST to the phob- family.
semantic_categories: contrast with commanded fear of God
word_studies.relevance: The OPPOSITE of the fear commanded in Rev 14:7. Deilos-fear (cowardice before circumstances) is condemned; phobeo-fear (reverence before God) is commanded. Rev 21:8 vs. Rev 14:7 — the cowardly perish while the God-fearers prevail.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to love/have affection for.' 225 occurrences across 27 books. Highest density in Hosea (11.6 per 100 verses). Covers covenantal love (Deut 6:5), God's love (Deut 7:8), interpersonal love, romantic love. In Deut 10:12, ahab appears THIRD in the sequence after yare and halak — love follows fear in the covenant pattern.
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew love-verb paired with yare (fear) in Deut 10:12. Fear and love appear as CO-REQUIREMENTS, not alternatives. Moskala claims biblical Hebrew preserves this integration — fear includes love.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun 'fear/terror/awesome thing.' 16 occurrences. From yare (H3372). Used for terrifying deeds (Deut 4:34, 26:8), dread of a superior (Gen 9:2), reverent fear due to God (Mal 1:6, Isa 8:13). In Isa 8:13, God should BE Israel's mora — their object of fear. More concrete than yirah — denotes the feared object rather than the inner disposition.
word_studies.relevance: Fills out the H3372 yare word family. Isa 8:13 ('let him be your mora') parallels Matt 10:28 — redirect fear from human threats to God himself.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to venerate/worship.' 10 occurrences (8 in Acts). Used primarily for 'God-fearers' — Gentiles who revered Israel's God (Acts 13:43, 16:14, 17:4). Also used for vain worship (Matt 15:9 / Mark 7:7 quoting Isa 29:13) and pagan worship (Acts 19:27). LXX maps from yare H3372 (3 co-occ).
word_studies.relevance: Part of the worship vocabulary alongside proskyneo and phobeo. The 'God-fearers' (sebomenoi) in Acts demonstrate what fearing God looks like in the Hellenistic world — the same category as Cornelius (Acts 10:2, using phobeo).

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun 'boldness/confidence/openness.' 31 verses across 11 books. From pas (all) + rhesis (speech) — 'all-outspokenness.' KJV 'boldness' 26%, 'confidence' 19%. In 1 John 4:17, parrhesia in the day of judgment is the FRUIT of perfected love — confidence replaces judicial fear.
word_studies.relevance: The POSITIVE counterpart to the fear cast out in 1 John 4:18. Perfect love produces parrhesia (boldness/confidence) in the day of judgment — replacing kolasin-fear with confident trust.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun 'lovingkindness/mercy/covenant faithfulness.' 241 verses, 255 tokens. Psalms dominates (127). The covenant-love that grounds the fear-love relationship. Paired with yirah 3x in collocations.
word_studies.relevance: The covenant-love context for the fear-love pairing. Prov 16:6 pairs checed/truth with yirah: 'by mercy and truth iniquity is purged; by the fear of the LORD men depart from evil.' God's checed is the GROUND for proper fear.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to judge/decide/condemn.' 98 verses, 132 tokens. Root of krisis (G2920). Active voice dominates (74%). Spans judicial, evaluative, and decisional senses.
word_studies.relevance: The verb behind krisis (judgment). The first angel announces that the hour of God's krisis has come — the same judgment process that 1 John 4:17 addresses.

word_studies.summary: Verb 'to love.' 109 verses, 147 tokens. Present active dominates (72 tokens). 11 imperative occurrences. In Deut 6:5 LXX: agapeseis = 'thou shalt love' — the love verb behind the greatest commandment.
word_studies.relevance: The verb form of agape. Deut 10:12 pairs yare (fear) with ahab (love) = phobeo with agapao in the LXX. Fear and love are co-required in the covenant.
